results.title: Experts Reveal the #1 Reason Lightning Rods Fail in Cincinnati Homes
results.content:
#### Spotting Physical Damage

 Bent or broken rods: Lightning rods that are bent or snapped due to heavy winds or hail will no longer be effective.
 Torn cables: If cables are frayed or snapped, they can’t conduct electricity properly.
 Damaged grounding system: If the grounding components are broken or disconnected, the lightning rod system won’t function properly.

### 5. Outdated Systems

Finally, another common issue is an outdated system. Over the years, lightning rod technology has improved, and older systems may not meet the current standards for safety and effectiveness. If your lightning protection system is more than 10-15 years old and hasn’t been upgraded, it may not be as effective at protecting your property as newer systems.

#### Signs Your System May Be Outdated [...] #### Signs Your System May Be Outdated

 Old materials: Older systems may use outdated or lower-quality materials that are more prone to wear and failure.
 Non-compliance with safety codes: Lightning rod standards have evolved over time. If your system doesn’t meet current safety codes, it might not provide adequate protection.

## The #1 Reason Lightning Rods Fail: Lack of Regular Maintenance and Inspections

While there are many factors that can contribute to common lightning rod issues in Cincinnati, OH, the number one reason lightning rods fail is a lack of regular maintenance and inspections. Just like any other safety system in your home or business, lightning rods need to be checked, maintained, and repaired regularly to ensure they function as intended. [...] #### Signs of Improper Installation

 Lightning rods are too short: The rods need to be tall enough to divert lightning strikes away from your home.
 Incorrect grounding: If the grounding system isn’t installed properly, the lightning strike may not be safely conducted into the earth.
 Disconnected or loose components: If parts of the system are not securely connected, it can cause the lightning to strike an unprotected area.

### 3. Lack of Maintenance

Lightning rods require regular maintenance to ensure they’re working efficiently. Over time, debris can accumulate on the rods, the system may become misaligned, or certain parts might degrade. Neglecting the system can lead to malfunctions, leaving your property vulnerable during storm season.

#### The Importance of Regular Inspections

results.title: Lightning Rods - National Weather Service
results.content:
National Weather Service
United States Department of Commerce

NWS

All NOAA

Safety

National Program

# Lightning Rods

lightning rods, see text for explanationLightning rods (and the accompanying protection system) are designed to protect a house or building from a direct lightning strike and, in particular, a lightning-initiated fire. Note that lightning protection systems do not prevent lightning from striking the structure, but rather intercept a lightning strike, provide a conductive path for the harmful electrical discharge to follow (the appropriate UL-listed copper or aluminum cable), and disperse the energy safely into the ground (grounding network). It's very important that these components be properly connected (bonded) to minimize the chances for any sparks or side flashes. [...] usa.gov

US Dept of Commerce  
National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration  
National Weather Service  
Safety  
 1325 East West Highway  
Silver Spring, MD 20910
  
  
  
Comments? Questions? Please Contact Us. [...] lightning rods, see text for explanation

While lightning rods help protect a structure from a direct lightning strike, a complete lightning protection system is needed to help prevent harmful electrical surges and possible fires caused by lightning entering a structure via wires and pipes. A complete system also includes electrical surge protection devices for incoming power, data, and communication lines; and surge protection devices for vulnerable appliances. Lightning protection may also be needed for gas piping.

Any lightning protection system should follow the national safety standards and requirements of the Lightning Protection Institute, National Fire Protection Association, and Underwriters Laboratories.
